text: Ah, Ryan Johnson. That's a name you
don't hear too often these days. Well,
except when he pops up occasionally to
subvert your expectations with another
trit, smug, borderline nonsensical
murder mystery film, which is the
cinematic equivalent of an Agatha
Christie novel filtered through the lens
of Gary Buucy.
Do I look like I need a psychological
evaluation?
Not at all. But there was a time, dear
viewer, when his disconcertingly round
head was on the thumbnail of every Star
Wars video on YouTube. There was a time
when his name was on the lips of every
fan of the franchise, usually followed
up by, "I can't believe they actually
let this guy make a Star Wars film."
Yes, the man who wrote and directed The
Last Jedi, arguably the single most
destructive and divisive movie in the
whole history of cinema. The film that
fractured an entire fan base, derailed
an already uncertain sequel trilogy,
indirectly triggered the downfall of
J.J. Abrams, put the entire Star Wars
franchise into a terminal decline, which
it's probably never going to recover
from, and gave us a scene that will live
in infamy for as long as cinema exists.
Yeah, that guy. The movie itself was bad
enough, but it was Ryan's attitude that
really seemed to rile people up. From
openly mocking the fan backlash and
sniping at people on social media with
passive aggressive clapbacks to casually
dismissing any criticism of The Last
Jedi, no matter how wellinformed or
intentioned, the guy just absolutely
wouldn't give an inch to anyone. The
film in his mind was literal perfection.
And if you couldn't see that, then you
were clearly too stupid to occupy the
same planet as his cosmic genius.
Really,
it wasn't just cope. It was an active
declaration of war against reality
itself. He almost seemed to get off on
the hatred directed towards him in his
film. Like his whole objective from the
start wasn't to entertain, but to
enrage. And well, mission accomplished,
I guess. Now, you might think that any
studio executive with even a grain of
common sense would see Johnson for the
nucleargrade liability that he was and
quietly shuffle him off into obscurity.
But leave it to everyone's favorite case
study for the Dunning Krueger effect,
Kathleen Kennedy, to take the most
logical course of action and do the
exact opposites.
Before The Last Jedi had even hit the
cinemas, Kennedy was hyping Johnson up
as the future of Star Wars. He wasn't
just doing this film, he was working on
a whole trilogy of movies that would
truly unleash his creative genius.
You sure about that?
You sure about that? And from her point
of view at least, you can kind of see
why he was so appealing at the time.
TLJ, for all its faults, was one of the
few Star Wars projects that didn't cause
her any major headaches during
production. The film arrived on time and
on budget with no major conflicts or
disasters to speak of. Johnson
apparently shares her political
sensibilities and her commitment to the
message. Plus, he seems like kind of a
[ __ ] so I'm sure he would have been
more than happy to implement all her
dumb as [ __ ] ideas. But even once The
Last Jedi released and the full
magnitude of the disaster became
obvious, even to the most disconnected
of executives, Kennedy doggedly stuck to
her guns, insisting that Ryan was her
man and his trilogy was absolutely going
to happen. Now, these days, the
announcement of a new Star Wars project
carries about as much weight as Ariana
Grande. But back in 2018, before
Lucasfilm had a list of failed movies
longer than my arm, this was still a big
deal, and people took it seriously. If
this roundheaded narcissist could
[ __ ] the franchise with just a single
film, imagine how much more damage he
could do with three of the [ __ ]
things.
It's a bold strategy, Cotton. Let's see
if it pays off for him.
But as would eventually become a
laughably predictable pattern for
Lucasfilm. Months slowly turned into
years and nothing more was heard about
Ryan's mythical trilogy. What was it
going to be about? What era would it be
set in? What characters would it deal
with? Was it even going to be in the
same galaxy as Star Wars? Nobody knew.
Least of all Kathleen Kennedy, who for
years continued to promise everyone who
would listen that Ryan was still working
on it, that it was all still going to
happen, that she wasn't desperately
throwing [ __ ] against the wall and
hoping that something would stick. Then
a few days ago, Ryan himself finally
confirmed what we all knew for years.
His trilogy was never going to happen.
Oh no. What? What happened?
Nothing really happened with it. We had
a great time working together and they
said, "Let's keep doing it." I said,
"Great. I would kick ideas around with
Kathy." The short version is Knives Out
happened. I went off and made Knives Out
and was off to the races, busy making
murder mysteries.
Yeah, I'm not really sure I buy the
whole I was just too busy excuse. Ryan's
released a grand total of two movies in
the past 7 years. He's not exactly
snowed under with work. It doesn't take
a genius to surmise what really happened
all those years ago. Everyone at
Lucasfilm was flying high in the run-up
to TLJ's release, expecting it to be an
even bigger hit than The Force Awakens.
The grand sequel that would finally bury
the legacy of old Star Wars and cement
the success of Kennedy's new vision for
the franchise. Then, when the exact
opposite happened, they were thrown into
panic modes. Even someone as stubbornly
[ __ ] as Kathleen Kennedy would have
to know that pushing ahead with a whole
trilogy of movies overseen by a guy less
popular than the [ __ ] Ebola virus was
something that even she couldn't afford
to indulge in. On the other hand,
publicly firing him or canceling his
trilogy would mean acknowledging the
failure of TLJ and by extension herself
for approving it in the first place. And
so instead, she did well, nothing at
all. She just kept kicking the can down
the road, vaguely alluding to the
trilogy still being worked on while
desperately hoping that people would
either forget about it or Ryan would
find something else to do. Any excuse
they could use for why his trilogy
couldn't happen. Oh yeah, he had amazing
ideas and we were all behind him and the
fans were super excited for more of
Ryan's version of Star Wars, but we just
couldn't get theuling to work out. What
can you do? E, are you serious? You
know, it's a testament to the lasting
damage that one movie did that even now,
almost a decade later, it's still such a
toxic and volatile subject that everyone
involved is still making excuses for it.
You'd think so long after the dust is
settled, Ryan himself might allow time
for just a little bit of introspection
and honesty about the film he made.
Maybe acknowledge that TLJ wasn't quite
the cinematic perfection they all
believed it to be, and that's why he's
never going to work in the Star Wars
franchise again. But nah, that would
involve showing a tiny bit of humility
and self-reflection. And just like 8
years ago when the backlash and
criticism first began, his ego just
won't allow for it. Better instead to
just plow ahead with your little murder
mystery films. I mean, the best thing I
can say about the Knives Out movies is
that, well, at least they keep him from
[ __ ] up any more franchises. So,
farewell, dear Ryan. Whatever anyone
else might say about you, you definitely
made your mark on Star Wars, that's for
sure.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
